coredata.dc:title: A graphene quantum dot@Fe3O4@SiO2 based nanoprobe for drug delivery sensing and dual-modal fluorescence and MRI imaging in cancer cells
coredata.dc:creator: Su, Xiaoqian
Chan, Chunyu
Shi, Jingyu
Tsang, Ming-Kiu
Pan, Yi
Cheng, Changming
Gerile, Oudeng
Yang, Mo
coredata.dc:description: A novel graphene quantum dot (GQD)@Fe3O4@SiO2 based nanoprobe was reported for targeted drug delivery, sensing, dual-modal imaging and therapy. Carboxyl-terminated GQD (C-GQD) was firstly conjugated with Fe3O4@SiO2 and then functionalized with cancer targeting molecule folic acid (FA). DOX drug molecules were then loaded on GQD surface of Fe3O4@SiO2@GQD-FA nanoprobe via pi-pi stacking, which resulted in Fe3O4@SiO2@GQD-FA/DOX conjugates based on a FRET mechanism with GQD as donor molecules and DOX as acceptor molecules. Meanwhile, we successfully performed in vitro MRI and fluorescence imaging of living Hela cells and monitored intracellular drug release process using this Fe3O4@SiO2@GQD-FA/DOX nanoprobe. Cell viability study demonstrated the low cytotoxicity of Fe3O4@SiO2@GQD-FA nanocarrier and the enhanced therapeutic efficacy of Fe3O4@SiO2@GQD-FA/DOX nanoprobe for cancer cells. This luminomagnetic nanoprobe will be a potential platform for cancer accurate diagnosis and therapy.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Graphene quantum dots (GQDs)
Fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET)
Magnetic resonance imaging (MRI)
Cell imaging
Drug delivery sensing

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: articleinfo articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pglast pii piinorm pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sectiontitle sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure e-component body mmlmath acknowledge affil appendices articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast footnotes grantnumber grantsponsor highlightsabst primabst ref

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of amino functionalized Fe3O4@SiO2 nanoparticles
Mechanism of nanoprobe based FRET sensing system
Characterizations of GQDs and the synthesis process of nanoprobe
Establishment of FRET system based on GQD and DOX
Intracellular imaging and drug release monitoring
Cell viability and therapeutic efficacy study

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Schematic illustration of the synthesis process of Fe3O4@SiO2@GQD-FA multifunctional nanoassembly. (b) Schematic illustration of DOX loading and release process on Fe3O4@SiO2@GQD-FA and the FRET system with GQD as donor molecule and DOX as acceptor molecule..

TEM images of (a) Fe3O4 nanoparticles, (b) GQDs, (c) Fe3O4@SiO2@GQD-FA; (d) UV–Vis spectra of GQD, FA, Fe3O4, Fe3O4@SiO2-NH2, Fe3O4@SiO2@GQD and Fe3O4@SiO2@GQD-FA; (e) FTIR spectra of GQD, Fe3O4, Fe3O4@SiO2-NH2, Fe3O4@SiO2@GQD and Fe3O4@SiO2@GQD-FA.

(a) PL spectra of GQDs, Fe3O4@SiO2@GQD, Fe3O4@SiO2@GQD-FA and Fe3O4@SiO2@GQD-FA/DOX. Inset: Photograph of the solution of GQDs taken under UV light. (b) PL spectra of Fe3O4@SiO2@GQD-FA with increasing molar ratio of DOX (from 0 to 20); (c) Relative DOX FRET signal change with concentration of DOX molecules; (d) Cumulative DOX release (%) from Fe3O4@SiO2@GQD-FA/DOX in PBS buffer with various pH values at different time intervals. Quantitative data are presented as mean±SD with n=3.

(a) M–H hysteresis loops of Fe3O4@SiO2@GQD-FA nanoparticles; (b) T2-weighted MRI of Fe3O4@SiO2@GQDs-FA at various Fe concentrations: 0.00, 0.04, 0.08, 0.12, and 0.16mM; (c) T2 relaxation rates of Fe3O4@SiO2@GQDs-FA at different Fe concentrations; (d) T2-weighted MRI of Hela cells incubated without and with Fe3O4@SiO2@GQDs-FA at Fe concentrations of 0.1mM for 24h.

Fluorescence images of HeLa cells treated with Fe3O4@SiO2@GQDs-FA/DOX at 0.5h, 6h, and 12h. GQDs channel (Ex: 405nm; Em: 475–505nm), DOX channel (Ex: 485nm; Em: 585–615nm), DOX FRET channel (Ex: 405nm; Em: 585–615nm).

(a) In-vitro cytotoxicity of Fe3O4@SiO2, Fe3O4@SiO2@GQD and Fe3O4@SiO2@GQD-FA and GQDs. (b) Viability profiles of DOX, Fe3O4@SiO2@GQD-DOX and Fe3O4@SiO2@GQD-FA/DOX after 24h incubation. Data were displayed as means±standard deviation with n=5.

head.ce:title: A graphene quantum dot@Fe3O4@SiO2 based nanoprobe for drug delivery sensing and dual-modal fluorescence and MRI imaging in cancer cells

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Interdisciplinary Division of Biomedical Engineering, the Hong Kong Polytechnic University, Hung Hom, Kowloon, Hong Kong, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Interdisciplinary Division of Biomedical Engineering, the Hong Kong Polytechnic University

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Applied Physics, the Hong Kong Polytechnic University, Hung Hom, Kowloon, Hong Kong, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Applied Physics, the Hong Kong Polytechnic University

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Sensing Technology and Biomedical Instruments, Sun Yat-Sen University, Guangzhou, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Sensing Technology and Biomedical Instruments, Sun Yat-Sen University

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors contributed equally to this work

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A novel graphene quantum dot (GQD)@Fe3O4@SiO2 based nanoprobe was reported for targeted drug delivery, sensing, dual-modal imaging and therapy. Carboxyl-terminated GQD (C-GQD) was firstly conjugated with Fe3O4@SiO2 and then functionalized with cancer targeting molecule folic acid (FA). DOX drug molecules were then loaded on GQD surface of Fe3O4@SiO2@GQD-FA nanoprobe via pi-pi stacking, which resulted in Fe3O4@SiO2@GQD-FA/DOX conjugates based on a FRET mechanism with GQD as donor molecules and DOX as acceptor molecules. Meanwhile, we successfully performed in vitro MRI and fluorescence imaging of living Hela cells and monitored intracellular drug release process using this Fe3O4@SiO2@GQD-FA/DOX nanoprobe. Cell viability study demonstrated the low cytotoxicity of Fe3O4@SiO2@GQD-FA nanocarrier and the enhanced therapeutic efficacy of Fe3O4@SiO2@GQD-FA/DOX nanoprobe for cancer cells. This luminomagnetic nanoprobe will be a potential platform for cancer accurate diagnosis and therapy.

ce:section.ce:para: The recent development of nanoprobes with multiple functionalities, including multimodal imaging, medical targeting, diagnostics, and therapeutics in a single system, has attracted considerable interests for various biomedical applications (Kelkar and Reineke, 2011; Jarzyna et al., 2010). The use of multifunctional nanoprobes for early diagnosis and therapy of diseases is expected to provide significant improvements on both diagnostic accuracy and therapeutic efficiency with minimized side effects. Functional fluorescent nanoparticles such as semiconductor quantum dots (QDs), have been used for bioimaging, diagnostics and therapy due to the good optical properties such as high quantum yield, low photobleaching and narrow photoluminescence bands (Bagalkot et al., 2007; Resch-Genger et al., 2008; Barroso, 2011). However, semiconductor QDs are toxic to cells due to the intrinsic heavy metal components. Moreover, the large size of QDs could interfere the intracellular functions (Valizadeh et al., 2012).

Graphene-based material is an especially popular class of multifunctional materials for biomedical applications due to its unique mechanical, thermal and optical properties, such as single-atom-layered structure amenable to modification (Lee et al., 2008), excellent thermal and optical adsorption capabilities (Balandin et al., 2008; Neto et al., 2009; Shi et al., 2015a), and low toxicity (Zhang et al., 2012). In particular, graphene quantum dots (GQDs), a new class of zero-dimensional graphene nanomaterial, exhibit tunable photoluminescence (PL) property, excellent photostability and great biocompatibility (Chen et al., 2012; Qian et al., 2014; Bhatnagar et al., 2016; Tian et al., 2016). Motivated by these significant features, GQDs are increasingly used as imaging and labelling probes in biomedical applications (Zheng et al., 2013; Shi et al., 2015b, 2016; Ge et al., 2014). Recently, GQDs have been used to carry therapeutic molecules for simultaneous cell imaging and therapeutic applications (Wang et al., 2013, 2014). Unlike conventional nanoscale drug carriers, GQDs exhibit an ultrahigh loading capacity for highly aromatic molecules via strong π-π stacking interactions, whose loading ratio is up to 4000% (w/w) (Some et al., 2014).

It is highly desirable to develop multimodal nanoprobes with selective targeting, dual-modal imaging, and real-time monitoring drug release process in cells. Multimodal imaging has gained great interest for improving diagnostic accuracy，which could not be achieved via just a single modality (Rieffel et al., 2015). Since magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) has advantages of deep penetration as well as high spatial resolution and fluorescence imaging has high sensitivity, a dual-imaging modality system combining fluorescence imaging and MRI will largely increase diagnostics efficiency (Zeng et al., 2014; Ma et al., 2015). However, there are no reports of GQD@Fe3O4 based nanoprobe with fluorescence/MRI dual-modal imaging and intracellular drug delivery sensing function.

In this paper, we report the development of a dual-imaging guided nanoprobe comprising GQDs attached on Fe3O4@SiO2 core/shell structure with tumor target molecule folic acid (FA) and anti-cancer drug doxorubicin (DOX) (Fe3O4@SiO2@GQD-FA/DOX), which could provide fluorescence/MRI dual imaging, deliver anti-cancer drugs to Hela cancer cells, and sense delivery process of drug molecules in the targeted tumor cells via fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) process. The cell viability experiments demonstrated the low cytotoxicity of Fe3O4@SiO2@GQD-FA nanocarrier. By loading DOX molecules, Fe3O4@SiO2@GQD-FA/DOX nanoprobes showed enhanced therapeutic efficacy compared with free DOX molecules.

Iron (III) chloride hexahydrate was purchased from Alfa Aesar. 1-Octadence (tech. 90%), Oleic acid (tech. 90%), tetraethyl orthosolicate (TEOS, 99%), 3-Aminopropyl triethoxysiliane (APTES, 98%), ethanol (99.5%), hexane (99%), citric acid (99.5%), Igepal CO-520, folic acid (97%), thiazolyl blue tetrazolium bromide (MTT) assay kit and DOX (98–102%) were purchased from Sigma Aldrich. Cyclohexane was acquired from Inthernational Laboratory. 1-(3-dimethylaminopropyl)-3-ethylcarbodiimide hydrochloride (EDC) and N-Hydroxysuxxinimide (NHS, 98%) were purchased from Tokyo Chemical Industry and J&K Scientific respectively. Dulbecco's modified Eagle's medium (DMEM), fluorobrite DMEM, fetal bovine serum (FBS), penicillin streptomycin, and trypsin-EDTA were obtained from Life Technologies. All solutions were prepared in DI water or phosphate buffer saline (PBS).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of amino functionalized Fe3O4@SiO2 nanoparticles
ce:section.ce:para: Fe3O4 nanoparticles were synthesized via the thermal decomposition method (Park et al., 2004). In a typical experiment, 1g of FeCl3·6H2O was dissolved in a mixture of 6mL of DI water, 8mL of ethanol and 14mL of hexane. Then 1.9mL of oleic acid and 0.24g NaOH were added and the mixture was kept at 70°C for 4h under vigorous stirring. After cooling down, the resultant iron–oleate complex was collected by separating funnel and washed three times with DI water. Finally, hexane was evaporated off after drying at 80°C overnight, resulting in iron–oleate complex in a waxy solid form. To obtain monodispersed Fe3O4 nanoparticles, 3.6g of iron–oleate precursor and 0.64mL of oleic acid were dissolved in 25mL of 1-octadecene and then degassed with argon for 30min at room temperature. The mixture solution was heated to 320°C and then kept at that temperature for 30min under argon flow. The resultant solution was then cooled to room temperature, and excess ethanol was added to the solution to precipitate the nanocrystals.

Core-shell Fe3O4@SiO2 nanoparticles were fabricated by a reverse microemulsion method (Ding et al., 2012). Typically, 5mg of as-prepared Fe3O4 nanoparticles were dispersed in a mixture of 2mL of Igapal CO-520 and 50mL of cyclohexane with continuous stirring, followed by adding 1mL of ammonia solution. Next, 0.06mL of TEOS was added dropwise and kept stirring for 12h. The resultant Fe3O4 nanoparticles were collected after magnetic separating and washing, and then re-dispersed in ethanol. After that, 0.06mL of APTES silane was introduced to obtain amino functionalized Fe3O4@SiO2 nanoparticles. The final product was collected via magnetic separation.

The GQDs were synthesized via directly pyrolyzing citric acid (Dong et al., 2012). Typically, 0.25g citric acid was heated to 200°C for 20min using an oven. Then the generated yellow liquid was added dropwise in 10mL of 0.25M NaOH solution. The blue luminescent GQD aqueous solution was obtained after NaOH neutralization. The conjugation of GQDs with amino functionalized Fe3O4@SiO2 nanoparticles was achieved by the classic carbodiimide crosslinking chemistry between carboxyl groups of GQDs and amine groups of Fe3O4@SiO2. Briefly, 80mg of EDC and 10mg of NHS was added to 5mL of GQDs solution (10mg/mL in PBS buffer) and incubated for 20min in dark environment. Subsequently, 1mg amino functionalized Fe3O4@SiO2 nanoparticles were added to conjugate with GQDs, followed by adding 50mg FA and 80mg NHS in 10mL MES buffer at room temperature. After 30min, the composites were collected by magnetic separation and then redispersed in PBS solution. The mixture was then stirred in the dark environment gently overnight, followed by centrifugation and filtering to remove the unreacted FA and NHS. The final product was then redispersed in PBS solution for further usage.

The shape, size and morphology of the synthesized nanoassembly were investigated with a JEOL-2100F transmission electron microscopy (TEM) equipped with an Oxford Instrument energy-dispersive X-ray (EDX) spectrometry system. Fourier transform infrared spectrum (FTIR) was collected using a PerkinElmer Spectrum 100 FT-IR spectrometer (PerkinElmer Inc., USA). UV–Vis spectra were performed on a UV/Visible spectrophotometer (Biochrom., England). Zeta potential measurements were performed on a Zetasizer Zeta Potential Analyzer (Malvern Instruments Ltd., England). Excitation and emission spectra were measured by a FLS920P Edinburgh Analytical Instrument. Confocal optical micrographs were obtained from a confocal laser scanning microscope (Leica SP8, Nikon, Japan).

The loading of DOX molecules onto Fe3O4@SiO2@GQD-FA was determined by measuring absorption at 480nm using a UV–visible spectrophotometer (Ultrospec 2100 Pro., GE). Generally, 5mg of DOX was mixed with 5mg of Fe3O4@SiO2@GQD-FA in 2mL of PBS buffer (pH=7.4). The mixture was further purified by magnetic separation after 24h stirring. Unbound DOX was quantified via analyzing the absorbance of the supernatant at the characteristic 480nm. The DOX drug loading efficiency (E
a ) is defined as the following equation (Xu et al., 2016):
E
a
=
C
0
−
C
1
C
0
Where, C
0 is the initial concentration of drug molecules, C
1 is the concentration of unbound drug molecules after adsorption. C0 and C1 are based on by measuring DOX concentrations at 480nm of absorption spectra after calibration.

The drug adsorption capacity Q is defined as the following equation:
Q =
(
C
0
−
C
1
) * V
m
Where, C0 is the initial concentration of drug molecules, C1 is the concentration of unbound drug molecules after adsorption. C0 and C1 are based on by measuring DOX concentrations at 480nm of absorption spectra after calibration. V is the volume of DOX solution and m is the mass of the sorbent.

All MRI images were collected in a clinical MR scanner (Siemens MAGNETO Trio, 3T, Germany) at room temperature. Fe3O4@SiO2@GQDs-FA at various Fe concentrations: 0.00, 0.04, 0.08, 0.12, and 0.16mM were suspended in 96 well plates for T2-weighted MRI imaging. For cell experiments, Hela cells were incubated with Fe3O4@SiO2@GQDs-FA with Fe concentration of 0.1mM in cell culture medium for 24h. After careful washing, Hela cells were then detached by trypsinization and centrifuged in a tube before imaging.

Hela cells with a concentration of 1×105 cells/mL were firstly incubated on Confocal Dish (Coverglass-Bottom Dish) for 24h. Solution with Fe3O4@SiO2@GQDs-FA/DOX (40µg/mL) was then added and co-cultured with Hela cells. After washing with PBS buffer twice, the Hela cells were monitored at various time points with the same optical conditions using confocal microscopy for the following channels: GQDs channel (Ex: 405nm; Em: 475–505nm), DOX channel (Ex: 485nm; Em: 585–615nm), DOX FRET channel (Ex: 405nm; Em: 585–615nm).

The viability of Hela cells exposed to Fe3O4@SiO2@GQD-FA/DOX, Fe3O4@SiO2@GQD-DOX, DOX, Fe3O4@SiO2, Fe3O4@SiO2@GQD, and Fe3O4@SiO2@GQD-FA was assessed by MTT assay. Hela cells were seeded at a density of 1×104/cell into 96-well plate. After 24h, the cells were exposed to various samples for another 24h of incubation. Then 20µL of MTT solution (5mg/mL) was added to each well and incubated at 37°C in 5% CO2 atmosphere for 4h. Next, the MTT solution was discarded and 200µL of DMSO was added to each well to dissolve formazan crystals. Absorbance of each sample was measured at 570nm using a microplate reader (Infinite F200, Tecan, Switzerland).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mechanism of nanoprobe based FRET sensing system
ce:section.ce:para: As shown in Fig. 1a, the Fe3O4@SiO2@GQD-FA/DOX nanoprobe is composed of four components: 1) Fe3O4@SiO2 core-shell structure for MRI imaging; 2) GQDs covalently conjugate with Fe3O4@SiO2 core-shell via amine-carboxyl linking, which serve a dual function of fluorescence probe and donor molecule in FRET; 3) Folic Acid (FA) conjugates with GQDs via amine-carboxyl linking, which serves as a targeting molecule for cancer cells; 4) Doxorubicin (DOX) with well-known fluorescent properties conjugates with GQDs via pi-pi stacking interaction, which serves a dual function of anti-cancer drug and acceptor molecule in FRET. The assembly of these components results in a FRET system, where the fluorescence emission of GQDs is quenched by the adsorption of DOX on GQD surface via pi-pi adsorption. At this assembly stage, GQD is at fluorescence “OFF” status and DOX is at fluorescence “ON” status under excitation. After nanoprobes are taken up by targeted cancer cells, DOX molecules are released from GQDs’ surface in the presence of acidic intracellular environment with the change of GQDs to “ON” status (Fig. 1b). Therefore, the localization of nanoprobes and the drug release process could be monitored via the change of FRET signals.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterizations of GQDs and the synthesis process of nanoprobe
ce:section.ce:para: Fe3O4 nanoparticles were synthesized with a thermal decomposition method, which showed good water dispersity ( Fig. 2a). The selected area electron diffraction (SAED) pattern confirmed the typical cubic spinel structure of Fe3O4 (Fig. S1a). Core-shell Fe3O4@SiO2 nanoparticles were then formed with a reverse microemulsion method. The prepared Fe3O4@SiO2 contained a core-shell structure with a 5nm of darker Fe3O4 core and 10nm of silica shell (Fig. S1b). The silica shell could avoid the possible fluorescence quenching effect by the Fe3O4 cores. The average size of synthesized Fe3O4@SiO2 nanoparticles was around 25nm (Fig. S1c). From the high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) image, the synthesized GQD showed good water dispersity with the lattice fringe around 0.2nm (Fig. 2b). The average size of GQDs was around 4nm (Fig. S1d). FA was then conjugated with GQD via carbodiimide crosslinking chemistry. The conjugation of GQDs-FA with Fe3O4@SiO2 composite led to the attachment of multiple GQDs on Fe3O4@SiO2 surface (Fig. 2c).

To further characterize the formation of Fe3O4@SiO2@GQD-FA, UV–Vis spectra of the nanoassembly formation were recorded (Fig. 2d). GQDs show an absorption peak at 325nm. The characteristic absorption peak of FA is around 280nm. For Fe3O4 nanoparticles and silica coated Fe3O4 nanoparticles, no obvious absorption peaks are observed in the spectra. After conjugation with GQDs and FA, the absorption spectrum of Fe3O4@SiO2@GQD-FA shows the characteristic peaks of GQDs and FA at 325nm and 280nm, respectively, indicating the successful formation of Fe3O4@SiO2@GQD-FA nanoassembly. Fig. 2e shows the Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) spectra of nanoassembly formation. FTIR of GQD shows peaks at 1725cm−1 and 1401cm−1 due to C=O and C-OH stretching vibration of –COOH groups. Other peaks are observed at 1628cm−1 and 1084cm−1 due to C=C and C-OH stretching vibrations. Fe3O4 nanoparticles show the characteristic Fe-O peak at 603cm−1. Wide bands at about 1645 and 3450cm−1 appear in all IR spectra, indicating the bending and stretching vibration of -OH. After coating silica shell on Fe3O4 core, two bands associated with Si-O-Si are observed at 1105cm−1 and 490cm−1, respectively, which present SiO2 shell on the surface of Fe3O4. The stretching vibration of -CH is observed at 2941cm−1 and 2870cm−1, presenting the successful conjugation of (3-aminopropyl)triethoxysilane (APTES) on Fe3O4@SiO2 surface. After covalent binding with GQDs, new peaks appear at 1340cm−1, which should be assigned to C–N stretching of EDC/NHS. Another characteristic band 1645cm−1 attributes to vibration of –NH–CO–, which indicates the formation of amide groups in Fe3O4@SiO2-GQD. Moreover, the zeta potential (ξ) of APTES modified Fe3O4@SiO2 is around +40mV due to the presence of NH2 + groups from silane molecules (Fig. S2). After conjugation with GQDs, the zeta potential is shifted to −25mV due to the abundant negative groups on GQD surface. The Zeta potential is further shifted to −18mV after FA conjugation.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Establishment of FRET system based on GQD and DOX
ce:section.ce:para: GQDs exhibit blue photoluminescence with emission peak at 460nm under UV excitation of 365nm (Fig. S3). DOX is a well-known fluorescent drug molecule with excitation peak around 470nm. The good overlapping between emission spectra of GQDs and excitation spectra of DOX makes it possible to establish a FRET system between GQDs and DOX. Fig. 3a shows the representative emission spectra of GQDs, Fe3O4@SiO2@GQD, Fe3O4@SiO2@GQD-FA and Fe3O4@SiO2@GQD-FA/DOX respectively. It shows that the emission peaks of GQD and Fe3O4@SiO2@GQD are around 460nm. The emission peak of Fe3O4@SiO2@GQD-FA is blue-shifted by ~25nm, suggesting the presence of FA on GQD surface. Upon DOX loading on GQD surface via pi-pi stacking interaction, the fluorescence signal of GQDs was quenched due to energy transfer from GQDs to DOX. Instead, an emission peak at 600nm by DOX was observed due to FRET effect. To further explore this FRET system, a fixed concentration of Fe3O4@SiO2@GQD-FA (1µM) was incubated with DOX at a series of increasing molar ratios. The sequential decrease of fluorescence signals of GQDs and the continuous increase of fluorescence signals of DOX were observed in the spectra, indicating the increasing adsorption of DOX molecules on GQD surface (Fig. 3b). The fluorescence decay curves of GOD before and after DOX adsorption were measured under 365nm laser pulse excitation (Fig. S4). The fluorescence lifetime of GQD was observed to be shortened after formation of GQD-DOX composites, which demonstrated that this process indeed induced non-radiative FRET effect. The adsorption of DOX on GQD surface could be detected by monitoring the DOX FRET signal with a limit of detection around 8ng/mL based on control group plus 3 times of standard derivation (Fig. 3c).

To explore the feasibility of Fe3O4@SiO2@GQD-FA as anti-tumor drug carriers, the drug adsorption capacity was determined by mixing 5mg DOX with 5mg Fe3O4@SiO2@GQD-FA in 2mL of PBS buffer (pH=7.4). The DOX concentration is measured based on the characteristic 480nm peak of DOX in UV–Vis spectra (Fig. S5). A 670mgg−1 drug adsorption capacity and 67% drug loading efficiency were achieved for Fe3O4@SiO2@GQD-FA/DOX probe. The cumulative DOX release profiles of Fe3O4@SiO2@GQD-FA/DOX under physiological condition (pH=7.4) and acidic condition (pH=5.5) were then monitored and shown in Fig. 3d. Fe3O4@SiO2@GQD-FA/DOX presented good stability under physiological conditions, with the release of only 7% of DOX at 6h and 18% of DOX at 24h. In contrast, 20% and 62% of DOX was speedily released at 6h and 24h in the acidic environment, respectively. After 3 days, up to 70% of DOX was released at pH=5.5. However, only 26% of DOX was released at pH=7.4 after 3 days. This pH-triggered drug release process of DOX could be explained by the increased protonated carboxyl and amino groups on DOX at lower pH environment, which weakened the binding force between DOX and GQDs (Niu et al., 2013; Liu et al., 2007, 2009). This pH-dependent DOX release property of this drug delivery system is highly needed for targeted cancer therapy, because tumor cells often exhibit an acidic environment (Gao et al., 2012; Ganta et al., 2008).

Fe3O4 nanoparticles have been proved to be an excellent T2 contrast agent for MRI (Zeng et al., 2014). Fig. 4a shows the M–H hysteresis loops of Fe3O4 nanoparticles before and after coating with silica and GQD, reflecting the characteristic superparamagnetic property of Fe3O4@SiO2@GQD. To evaluate the potential of Fe3O4@SiO2@GQD-FA as diagnostic MR contrast agents, the MR signals of Fe3O4@SiO2@GQD-FA at different Fe concentrations were measured by a 3T MRI scanner (Fig. 4b). The results indicated that T2-weighted signal intensity decreased significantly with the increase of Fe concentrations, which was attributed to the dipolar interaction between magnetic moments of Fe3O4 and protons in water. The spin-spin relaxation time was thus shortened, leading to the darker images (Ma et al., 2015). Fig. 4c shows the transverse (1/T2) relaxation rates of Fe3O4@SiO2@GQD-FA as a function of Fe concentrations in de-ionized water. The results showed a linear relationship between relaxation rates and Fe concentrations with a high relaxation value of 62.8mM−1 s−1, indicating the as-prepared Fe3O4@SiO2@GQD-FA was feasible for efficient T2-weighted MRI. The feasibility of Fe3O4@SiO2@GQD-FA nanoassembly for in-vitro MRI in Hela cells was then investigated. Fig. 4d clearly showed the enhancement of negative image contrast of Hela cells after incubation with Fe3O4@SiO2@GQD-FA (equivalent Fe concentration of 0.1mM) for 24h compared with the bright image of untreated cells.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Intracellular imaging and drug release monitoring
ce:section.ce:para: To explore the functionality of Fe3O4@SiO2@GQD-FA/DOX nanoprobe for intracellular imaging and drug delivery sensing in vitro, the uptake of nanoprobes and change of FRET signals in Hela cells were investigated via fluorescence microscopy over extended time periods. The cell samples were washed carefully with PBS solution several times to remove unbound nanoprobes before fluorescence images were taken. As shown in Fig. 5, after 0.5h incubation with Fe3O4@SiO2@GQD-FA/DOX nanoprobes, red fluorescence signal (DOX FRET channel) was observed at the boundary region of Hela cells, indicating the start of entry of nanocarriers into Hela cells. At this initial stage, the fluorescence of GQDs was largely at the “OFF” status (GQD channel), indicating that DOX molecules were mostly stacked on the surface of GQDs. After 6h of incubation, an obvious increase of blue fluorescence (GQD channel) was observed in cell cytoplasm with “ON” status due to the detachment of DOX molecules from the carriers. The FRET signals were mainly in the cell cytoplasm, whereas red fluorescence of DOX under direct excitation (DOX channel, Ex: 485nm) was observed in both cytoplasm and nuclei. Since FRET signals mainly represented unleashed DOX molecules on carriers, it was concluded that some released DOX molecules entered into nuclei and nanoprobes with unreleased DOX molecules were mainly in the cell cytoplasm. After 12h incubation, strong red fluorescence could be observed in cell nuclei (DOX 485 ex channel) with a decrease of FRET signals (DOX FRET channel), which indicated most DOX molecules were released from carriers and accumulated inside nuclei. Blue fluorescence was also observed inside cell nuclei (GQD channel), suggesting that some nanoprobes entered into cell nuclei at this stage. The results demonstrated that this nanoprobe was sensitive to detect intracellular drug release process on a single cancer cell level in vitro.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cell viability and therapeutic efficacy study
ce:section.ce:para: To confirm the biocompatibility of the nano-carriers, the cytotoxicity of Fe3O4@SiO2, Fe3O4@SiO2@GQD, and Fe3O4@SiO2@GQD-FA on Hela cells were studied with an MTT assay. All samples show excellent biocompatibility with cell viability larger than 80% and Fe3O4@SiO2@GQD-FA nano-carrier showed good cell viability up to 100µg/mL ( Fig. 6a). Therapeutic efficacy was also compared among pure DOX, Fe3O4@SiO2@GQD/DOX and Fe3O4@SiO2@GQD-FA/DOX for a series of DOX concentrations. As shown in Fig. 6b, Hela cells showed lower viability with Fe3O4@SiO2@GQD-FA/DOX and Fe3O4@SiO2@GQD/DOX compared with that of pure DOX and for all the concentrations, indicating the largely increased therapeutic efficiency with nanoassembly drug carriers. Moreover, for a DOX concentration of 10µg/mL, the cell viability with Fe3O4@SiO2@GQD-FA/DOX (15.4%) was even lower than Fe3O4@SiO2@GQD-DOX (20.4%), which could be contributed to the enhanced targeting efficiency to Hela cells of FA modified Fe3O4@SiO2@GQD compared to that of non-targeted Fe3O4@SiO2@GQD carriers.

MRI/fluorescent bimodal molecular imaging could provide both high-resolution information of deep tissue and high-sensitivity functional imaging, which is a powerful tool for accurate molecular diagnostics and therapeutics. Most of the current nanoprobes for MRI/fluorescent imaging are based on semiconductor quantum dots (QDs) and magnetic nanoparticles (Mulder et al., 2006; Shibu et al., 2013). However, semiconductor QDs containing heavy metals suffer from the intrinsic toxicity, which prevents their wide applications in biomedical field. Magnetic nanoparticle/QDs nanocomposites have also been used for simultaneous imaging and drug delivery. However, both magnetic nanoparticles and semiconductor QDs are lack of drug loading sites, which need an additional coating layer of other materials for drug loading such as graphene (Ou et al., 2014) or poly(lactic-co-glycolic acid) (PLGA) (Cho et al., 2010). Compared with semiconductor QDs, GQDs have much lower toxicity and plenty of drug loading sites, which could be simultaneously used as biocompatible fluorescence probes and efficient drug carriers. Therefore, GQDs/Fe3O4 multifunctional probes are more suitable for long-term cellular monitoring without the need of additional drug loading layer. Generally, this GQDs/Fe3O4 luminomagnetic nanoprobe, characterized by excellent biocompatibility, real-time drug delivery monitoring, dual modality fluorescence and MRI imaging and enhanced targeted cancer therapeutic efficacy, would be a potential platform for accurate cancer diagnosis and therapy.

In summary, Fe3O4@SiO2@GQD-FA/DOX multifunctional nanoprobes have been developed with fluorescence/MRI imaging and FRET based drug release sensing. By monitoring the change of FRET signals in the cancer cells, the cellular uptake of nanoprobes and intracellular drug molecule release process were successfully monitored. Moreover, the therapeutic efficacy of DOX could be enhanced by conjugation with the Fe3O4@SiO2@GQD-FA nanocarriers. Overall, this GQD@Fe3O4 based multifunctional luminomagnetic nanoprobe could be a promising platform for targeted cancer accurate diagnosis and therapy with drug molecule tracking function.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the Hong Kong Research Council General Research Grant (PolyU 152213/15E), the Hong Kong Ph. D Fellowship Scheme Fund (HKPF14-14076 and HKPF15-17095) and the internal fund of the Hong Kong Polytechnic University (G-YBAA and 1-YW1L).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.bios.2016.10.076.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
